question: Statement: 'Advertise your product in our newspaper for wide reach.' Assumption: People read the newspaper.
solution: Negation: if no one reads it, there is no reach — the statement fails. So it is implicit.

question: Statement: 'Please switch off the lights when you leave the room.' Assumption: People are able to switch off the lights.
solution: If people can't switch them off, the appeal is pointless — so it is implicit.

question: Statement: 'This medicine cures fever within three days.' Assumption: The medicine cures fever.
solution: The cure is explicitly stated, so it is not an assumption.

question: Statement: 'Our coaching improved many students' scores.' Assumption: All students who join will top the exam.
solution: 'Many improved' doesn't require 'all will top' — an extreme, unnecessary claim.

question: Statement: 'The city will run extra buses to reduce traffic.' Assumption: Extra buses can help reduce traffic.
solution: If extra buses can't reduce traffic, the plan is pointless — so it is implicit.

question: Statement: 'Book your tickets online to avoid queues.' Assumption: Online booking is available.
solution: The advice only makes sense if online booking exists — implicit.

question: Statement: 'Attend our seminar to learn new investment strategies.' Assumption: The seminar will cover investment strategies.
solution: The promise implies the seminar's content covers those strategies — implicit.

question: Statement: 'The manager asked employees to submit reports by email.' Assumption: Employees have access to email.
solution: The instruction assumes employees can use email — negation breaks it.

question: Statement: 'Visit our store this weekend for exclusive discounts.' Assumption: Customers prefer discounts.
solution: The offer relies on discounts attracting customers — implicit.

question: Statement: 'The college will start evening classes for working students.' Assumption: Some working people wish to study.
solution: Starting evening classes for them assumes such demand exists — implicit.
trick: Service assumes a need for it.

question: Statement: 'Use our app to track your daily steps.' Assumption: Users own a device that runs the app.
solution: Using the app requires a compatible device — implicit.

question: Statement: 'The notice says the office will be closed on Sunday.' Assumption: The office normally operates on other days.
solution: Announcing a Sunday closure implies it is otherwise open — implicit.

question: Statement: 'Everyone in the country supports the new law.' Assumption: The law is popular with a few people.
solution: The claim is itself stated (and extreme); 'a few' is not the assumption behind it.

question: Statement: 'Drink our juice daily to stay healthy.' Assumption: The juice contributes to health.
solution: The claim relies on the juice benefiting health — implicit.

question: Statement: 'The bank introduced a mobile alert service for every transaction.' Assumptions: I. Customers have mobile phones. II. Customers want to be alerted. Which is implicit?
solution: The service needs customers to have phones (I) and presumes they value alerts (II); both are implicit.

question: Statement: 'Government urges citizens to save water this summer.' Assumption: Citizens can reduce their water use.
solution: An appeal to save water assumes people are able to cut usage — implicit.

question: Statement: 'The publisher printed 10,000 extra copies of the book.' Assumption: There is demand for the extra copies.
solution: Printing extra copies assumes demand for them — negation makes the decision irrational.

question: Statement: 'The airline added a direct flight between the two cities.' Assumption: Some people travel between the two cities.
solution: Adding the route assumes travellers between the cities exist — implicit.

question: Statement: 'Read the instructions carefully before using the device.' Assumption: The user can read the instructions.
solution: The instruction assumes the user is able to read them — implicit.

question: Statement: 'The company will give a bonus only if profits exceed last year.' Assumption: The company made a profit last year.
solution: Comparing to last year's profit assumes there was a profit to exceed — implicit.
